story: Finalist Phillip Phillips suffered from kidney pain and was taken to the hospital before the Top 13 results show, and later received medical procedure to alleviate a blockage caused by kidney stones. He was reported to have eight surgeries during his Idol run, and had considered quitting the show due to the pain. He underwent surgery to remove the stones and reconstruct his kidney soon after the season had finished.
q1: Phillip Phillips had eight surgeries during his time on American Idol. (true/false)
q2: Phillip Phillips was hospitalized before the Top 10 results show due to kidney stones. (true/false)

story: Jessica Sanchez received the fewest number of votes during the Top 7 week, and the judges decided to use their "save" option on her, making her the first female recipient of the save. The following week, unlike previous seasons, Colton Dixon was the only contestant sent home. Sanchez later made the final two, the first season where a recipient of the save reached the finale.
q1: Jessica Sanchez was the first female contestant saved by the Judges' Save on American Idol. (true/false)
q2: Jessica Sanchez received the fewest votes during the Top 9 week. (true/false)

story: 23-year-old Candice Glover won the season with Kree Harrison taking the runner-up spot. Glover is the first female to win American Idol since Jordin Sparks. Glover released "I Am Beautiful" as a single while Harrison released "All Cried Out" immediately after the show. Glover sold poorly with her debut album, and this is also the first season that the runner-up was not signed by a music label.
q1: Candice Glover is the first female to win American Idol since Jordin Sparks. (true/false)
q2: Kree Harrison was signed by a music label following season 12 of American Idol. (true/false)

story: Towards the end of the season, Randy Jackson, the last remaining of the original judges, announced that he would no longer serve as a judge to pursue other business ventures. Both judges Mariah Carey and Nicki Minaj also decided to leave after one season to focus on their music careers.
q1: Randy Jackson was the last of the original judges remaining on American Idol. (true/false)
q2: Mariah Carey and Nicki Minaj both departed after serving two seasons as judges. (true/false)

story: This was the first season where the contestants were permitted to perform in the final rounds songs they wrote themselves. In the Top 8, Sam Woolf received the fewest votes, but he was saved from elimination by the judges. The 500th episode of the series was the Top 3 performance night.
q1: The 500th episode of the series was the Top 5 performance night. (true/false)
q2: Season 13 was the first season where contestants could perform their own compositions in the final rounds. (true/false)

story: The fourteenth season premiered on January 7, 2015. Ryan Seacrest returned to host, while Jennifer Lopez, Keith Urban and Harry Connick, Jr. returned for their respective fourth, third and second seasons as judges. Eighth season runner-up Adam Lambert filled in for Urban during the New York City auditions. Randy Jackson did not return as the in-house mentor for this season.
q1: Adam Lambert filled in for Keith Urban during the New York City auditions in season 14. (true/false)
q2: Randy Jackson returned as the in-house mentor for season 14. (true/false)

story: Fox announced on May 11, 2015 that the fifteenth season would be the final season of American Idol; as such, the season is expected to have an additional focus on the program's alumni. Ryan Seacrest returns as host, with Harry Connick Jr., Keith Urban, and Jennifer Lopez all returning for their respective third, fourth, and fifth seasons as judges.
q1: Jennifer Lopez served as a judge for her fifth season of American Idol in season 15. (true/false)
q2: Fox announced the cancellation of American Idol on May 11, 2014. (true/false)

story: The show's massive success in the mid-2000s and early 2010s spawned a number of imitating singing-competition shows, such as Rock Star, Nashville Star, The Voice, Rising Star, The Sing-Off, and The X Factor. Its format also served as a blueprint for non-singing TV shows such as Dancing with the Stars and So You Think You Can Dance, most of which contribute to the current highly competitive reality TV landscape on American television.
q1: The format of American Idol served as a blueprint for other singing competition shows only. (true/false)
q2: The Voice and The X Factor are among the singing-competition shows spawned by American Idol's success. (true/false)

story: As one of the most successful shows on U.S. television history, American Idol has a strong impact not just on television, but also in the wider world of entertainment. It helped create a number of highly successful recording artists, such as Kelly Clarkson, Daughtry and Carrie Underwood, as well as others of varying notability.
q1: American Idol's impact has been limited to the television industry. (true/false)
q2: Kelly Clarkson and Carrie Underwood are among the successful recording artists created by American Idol. (true/false)

story: Coca-Cola's archrival PepsiCo declined to sponsor American Idol at the show's start. What the Los Angeles Times later called "missing one of the biggest marketing opportunities in a generation" contributed to Pepsi losing market share, by 2010 falling to third place from second in the United States. PepsiCo sponsored the American version of Cowell's The X Factor in hopes of not repeating its Idol mistake until its cancellation.
q1: PepsiCo fell from second to third place in U.S. market share by 2010 after declining to sponsor American Idol. (true/false)
q2: PepsiCo sponsored American Idol in its first season after initially declining. (true/false)

story: Individual contestants have generated controversy in this competition for their past actions, or for being 'ringers' planted by the producers. A number of contestants had been disqualified for various reasons, such as for having an existing contract or undisclosed criminal records, although the show had been accused of double standard for disqualifying some but not others.
q1: All contestants found to have prior recording contracts were treated equally under the show's rules. (true/false)
q2: Some contestants have been disqualified for having undisclosed criminal records. (true/false)

story: Seasonal rankings (based on average total viewers per episode) of American Idol. It holds the distinction of having the longest winning streak in the Nielsen annual television ratings; it became the highest-rated of all television programs in the United States overall for an unprecedented seven consecutive years, or eight consecutive (and total) years when either its performance or result show was ranked number one overall.
q1: American Idol held the top position in the Nielsen ratings for six consecutive years. (true/false)
q2: American Idol holds the longest winning streak in the Nielsen annual television ratings. (true/false)

story: Dogs perform many roles for people, such as hunting, herding, pulling loads, protection, assisting police and military, companionship, and, more recently, aiding handicapped individuals. This impact on human society has given them the nickname "man's best friend" in the Western world. In some cultures, however, dogs are a source of meat.
q1: Dogs are used as a source of meat in all cultures. (true/false)
q2: Dogs have been given the nickname 'man's best friend' in the Western world. (true/false)

story: Domestic dogs have been selectively bred for millennia for various behaviors, sensory capabilities, and physical attributes. Modern dog breeds show more variation in size, appearance, and behavior than any other domestic animal. Dogs are predators and scavengers, and like many other predatory mammals, the dog has powerful muscles, fused wrist bones, a cardiovascular system that supports both sprinting and endurance, and teeth for catching and tearing.
q1: Modern dog breeds show more variation in size, appearance, and behavior than any other domestic animal. (true/false)
q2: Dogs have a cardiovascular system optimized for sprinting only, not endurance. (true/false)

story: Domestic dogs often display the remnants of countershading, a common natural camouflage pattern. A countershaded animal will have dark coloring on its upper surfaces and light coloring below, which reduces its general visibility. Thus, many breeds will have an occasional "blaze", stripe, or "star" of white fur on their chest or underside.
q1: Countershading makes the upper surfaces of a dog light-colored and the underside dark. (true/false)
q2: Countershading reduces the general visibility of an animal. (true/false)

story: The breed with the shortest lifespan (among breeds for which there is a questionnaire survey with a reasonable sample size) is the Dogue de Bordeaux, with a median longevity of about 5.2 years, but several breeds, including Miniature Bull Terriers, Bloodhounds, and Irish Wolfhounds are nearly as short-lived, with median longevities of 6 to 7 years.
q1: The breed with the shortest lifespan is the Irish Wolfhound. (true/false)
q2: The median longevity of the Dogue de Bordeaux is about 5.2 years. (true/false)

story: Dogs bear their litters roughly 58 to 68 days after fertilization, with an average of 63 days, although the length of gestation can vary. An average litter consists of about six puppies, though this number may vary widely based on the breed of dog. In general, toy dogs produce from one to four puppies in each litter, while much larger breeds may average as many as twelve.
q1: The average gestation period for dogs is 63 days. (true/false)
q2: An average dog litter consists of about ten puppies. (true/false)

story: Neutering reduces problems caused by hypersexuality, especially in male dogs. Spayed female dogs are less likely to develop some forms of cancer, affecting mammary glands, ovaries, and other reproductive organs. However, neutering increases the risk of urinary incontinence in female dogs, and prostate cancer in males, as well as osteosarcoma, hemangiosarcoma, cruciate ligament rupture, obesity, and diabetes mellitus in either sex.
q1: Neutering eliminates all health risks for both male and female dogs. (true/false)
q2: Spayed female dogs are less likely to develop cancers affecting the mammary glands and ovaries. (true/false)

story: Dog communication is about how dogs "speak" to each other, how they understand messages that humans send to them, and how humans can translate the ideas that dogs are trying to transmit.:xii These communication behaviors include eye gaze, facial expression, vocalization, body posture (including movements of bodies and limbs) and gustatory communication (scents, pheromones and taste). Humans communicate with dogs by using vocalization, hand signals and body posture.
q1: Humans communicate with dogs through vocalization, hand signals, and body posture. (true/false)
q2: Gustatory communication in dogs involves eye gaze and facial expression. (true/false)

story: Purebred dogs of one breed are genetically distinguishable from purebred dogs of other breeds, but the means by which kennel clubs classify dogs is unsystematic. Systematic analyses of the dog genome has revealed only four major types of dogs that can be said to be statistically distinct. These include the "old world dogs" (e.g., Malamute and Shar Pei), "Mastiff"-type (e.g., English Mastiff), "herding"-type (e.g., Border Collie), and "all others" (also called "modern"- or "hunting"-type).
q1: Kennel clubs classify dogs using a systematic and scientifically consistent method. (true/false)
q2: Genetic analyses of the dog genome have revealed four major statistically distinct types. (true/false)
